titel: Adaptations to the EU's regulations regarding the management of financial institutions in crisis situations.
html:
The EU's crisis management directive, implemented through the law on resolution, contains regulations for handling financial institutions in crisis through resolution, i.e., liquidation or restructuring. One of the key tools is the write-down or conversion of liabilities. For companies to have liabilities that can be written down or converted, they must meet requirements for capital adequacy and qualifying liabilities. According to amendments to the EU's crisis management directive, resolution entities, such as companies that are to be declared bankrupt or liquidated instead of undergoing resolution, are generally exempt from the requirements for capital adequacy and qualifying liabilities. These amendments to the directive also mean that liabilities issued by such companies should not be deducted when calculating how a resolution group consisting of several credit institutions meets the requirements for capital adequacy and qualifying liabilities. Furthermore, certain subsidiaries must meet the requirements for capital adequacy and qualifying liabilities at the consolidated level. The government proposal includes the legislative proposals necessary to implement the amending directive into Swedish law.

The government proposal also suggests clarifying certain provisions in the law on resolution, following concerns raised by the European Commission regarding whether certain articles of the EU's crisis management directive (regardless of the amending directive) have been fully implemented into Swedish law. These articles regulate, among other things, how the requirements for capital adequacy and qualifying liabilities should be determined and met.

The proposed amendments to the law are scheduled to come into effect on November 1, 2025.

Proposed resolutions for the Parliament

Prop. 2024/25:172

Government Proposal: The Riksdag (Swedish Parliament) shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act (2015:1016) on Resolution. 5

Prop. 2024/25:172 2 Proposed Act Amending the Act (2015:1016) on Resolution

Hereby, the following is stipulated with regard to the Act (2015:1016) on Resolution: (a) Sections 2 Chapter 1, 3 Chapter 12 and 17, 4 Chapter 1, 3, 4, 9, 10, 12, 14, 18, 20, 21, 23, 28, 32-34 and 38, 5 Chapter 3a and 9, 6 Chapter 1 and 14, 21 Chapter 28 and 29 Chapter 2 shall have the following wording; (b) Four new sections, 4 Chapter 22a and 23a-23c, with the following wording, shall be introduced.

Current Wording | Proposed Wording ---|--- 2 Chapter. 1 § 2 | In this Act, the following terms shall have the following meanings: "notified settlement system": a notified settlement system according to Section 2 of the Act (1999:1309) on a system for the settlement of obligations in the financial market, "settlement unit": an undertaking that is not a resolution unit and which (a) according to a resolution or group resolution plan, shall be wound up through bankruptcy or liquidation, or (b) according to a group resolution plan, shall not be subject to write-down or conversion according to Chapters 6 or 21, "business day": a day that is not a Saturday, a Sunday, Midsummer's Eve, Christmas Eve, or New Year's Eve, or a public holiday, "competent authority": in Sweden, the Financial Supervisory Authority, and in other countries within the EEA, a competent authority according to the definition in Article 4.1.40 of the supervisory regulation, including the European Central Bank with regard to the specific tasks assigned to it by Council Regulation (EU) No 1024/2013 of 15 October 2013 on the tasks of the European Central Bank in the area of supervision of credit institutions, in its original wording, "relevant authority": in Sweden, the Financial Supervisory Authority, and in other countries within the EEA, the authority that the countries have identified according to Article 61.2 of the crisis management directive, "significant branch": a branch that is significant according to Article 51.1 of the capital requirements directive, "mixed financial holding company": a holding company according to Article 4.1.21 of the supervisory regulation,

1 See European Parliament and Council Directive (EU) 2024/1174 of 11 April 2024 amending Directive 2014/59/EU and Regulation (EU) No 806/2014 as regards certain aspects of the minimum requirement for own funds and eligible liabilities, in its original wording.
tldr: Sweden is changing the regulations governing how banks and financial institutions are handled in the event of a crisis. The purpose is to comply with new rules from the EU, making it clearer what requirements apply to different types of companies.
lattlast:
The government proposes new laws to align Sweden with the EU's regulations on crisis management of financial institutions. This concerns what happens to banks and other large companies when they can no longer operate normally and need to be either rescued or shut down.

A key element of these regulations is the requirement for capital and liabilities. For a company to be eligible for rescue during a crisis, it needs to have sufficient capital and specific types of liabilities that can be written off. The new rules clarify that companies that are destined for bankruptcy or closure do not need to meet the same strict capital requirements.

The proposal also includes clarifications on how to calculate the amount of capital that different companies within a group need to hold. This is to avoid misunderstandings and to ensure that the Swedish regulations are consistent with the requirements set by the European Commission.

If the Parliament votes in favor of the proposal, the new laws will come into effect on November 1, 2025. These changes will result in a more equitable distribution of requirements between companies that are to be rescued and those that are to be wound down.
